SECTION I
CONTRACT CLAUSES
I.1 252.204-7021 CONTRACTOR COMPLIANCE WITH THE CYBERSECURITY MATURITY MODEL CERTIFICATION LEVEL
REQUIREMENTS (NOV 2025)
(a) Definitions. As used in this clause-
Controlled unclassified information means information the Government creates or possesses, or
information an entity creates or possesses for or on behalf of the Government, that a law, regulation,
or Governmentwide policy requires or permits an agency to handle using safeguarding or dissemination
controls (32 CFR 2002.4 (h))
Current means--
(1) With regard to Conditional Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) Status--
(i) Not older than 180 days for Conditional Level 2 (Self) assessments and Conditional Level 2
(certified third-party assessment organization (C3PAO) ) assessments, with--
(A) No changes in compliance with the requirements at 32 CFR part 170 since the Conditional CMMC
Status date (see 32 CFR 170.16 and 170.17); and
(B) A corresponding affirmation of continuous compliance by an affirming official (see 32 CFR 170.4)
and
(ii) Not older than 180 days for Conditional Level 3 (Defense Industrial Base Cybersecurity Assessment
Center (DIBCAC)) assessments, with--
(A) No changes in compliance with the requirements at 32 CFR part 170 since the Conditional CMMC
Status date (see 32 CFR 170.18) and
(B) A corresponding affirmation of continuous compliance by an affirming official;
(2) With regard to Final CMMC Status--
(i) Not older than 1 year for Final Level 1 (Self), with--
(A) No changes in compliance with the requirements at 32 CFR part 170 since the Final CMMC Status date
(see 32 CFR 170.15) ; and
(B) A corresponding affirmation of continuous compliance, not older than 1 year, by an affirming
official;
(ii) Not older than 3 years for Final Level 2 (Self) assessments and Final Level 2 (C3PAO)
assessments, with--
(A) No changes in compliance with the requirements at 32 CFR part 170 since the Final CMMC Status date
(see 32 CFR 170.16 and 170.17);
and
(B) A corresponding affirmation of continuous compliance, not older than 1 year, by an affirming
official; and
(iii) Not older than 3 years for Final Level 3 (DIBCAC) assessments, with--
(A) No changes in compliance with the requirements at 32 CFR part 170 since the Final CMMC Status date
(see 32 CFR 170.18) and
(B) A corresponding affirmation of continuous compliance, not older than 1 year, by an affirming
official; and
(3) With regard to affirmation of continuous compliance (32 CFR 170.22), not older than 1 year with no
changes in compliance with the requirements at 32 CFR part 170.
Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) status means the result of meeting or exceeding the
minimum required score for the corresponding assessment. The potential statuses are as follows:
(1) Final Level 1 (Self).
(2) Conditional Level 2 (Self).
(3) Final Level 2 (Self).
(4) Conditional Level 2 (C3PAO)
(5) Final Level 2 (C3PAO).
(6) Conditional Level 3 (DIBCAC).
(7) Final Level 3 (DIBCAC).
Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification unique identifier (CMMC UID) means 10 alpha-numeric
characters assigned to each CMMC assessment and reflected in the Supplier Performance Risk System (SPRS)
for each contractor information system.
Federal contract information (FCI) means information, not intended for public release, that is
provided by or generated for the Government under a contract to develop or deliver a product or service
to the Government. It does not include information provided by the Government to the public, such as on
public websites, or simple transactional information, such as information necessary to process payments.
(d) Requirements. The Contractor shall--
Plan of action and milestones means a document that identifies tasks to be accomplished. It details
resources required to accomplish the elements of the plan, any milestones in meeting the tasks, and
scheduled completion dates for the milestones, as defined in National Institute of Standards and
Technology Special Publication 800-115 (32 CFR 170.21).
UNCLASSIFIED
PAGE 3 OF 56 HHM40225R0025/
UNCLASSIFIED
(b) Framework. The Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) is a framework for assessing a
contractor's compliance with applicable information security protections (see 32 CFR part 170).
(c) Duplication. The CMMC assessments will not duplicate efforts from any other comparable DoD
assessment, except for rare circumstances when a reassessment may be necessary, for example, when there
are indications of issues with cybersecurity and/or compliance with CMMC requirements.
(1) (i) Have and maintain for the duration of the contract a current CMMC status at the following CMMC
level, or
higher: [Contracting Officer insert: CMMC Level 1 (Self); CMMC Level 2 (Self); CMMC Level 2
(C3PAO) ; or CMMC Level 3 (DIBCAC) ] for all information systems used in performance of the contract, task
order, or delivery order that process, store, or transmit FCI or CUI; and
(ii) Consult 32 CFR 170.23 related to the flowdown of the CMMC requirements, and flow down the correct
CMMC level to subcontracts and other contractual instruments;
(2) Only process, store, or transmit FCI or CUI on contractor information systems that have a CMMC
status at the CMMC level required in paragraph (d) (1) of this clause, or higher;
(3) Complete on an annual basis, and maintain as current, an affirmation, by the affirming official
(see 32 CFR 170.4), of continuous compliance with the requirements associated with the CMMC level
required in paragraph (d) (1) of this clause in the Supplier Performance Risk System (SPRS) (https://
piee.eb.mil) for each CMMC VID applicable to each of the contractor information systems that process,
store, or transmit FCI or CUI and that are used in performance of the contract;
(4) Ensure all subcontractors and suppliers complete prior to subcontract award, and maintain on an
annual basis, an affirmation, by the affirming official (see 32 CFR 170.4), of continuous compliance
with the requirements associated with the CMMC level required for the subcontract or other contractual
instrument for each of the subcontractor information systems that process, store, or transmit FCI or CUI
and that are used in performance of the subcontract; and
(5) If the Contractor has a CMMC Status of Conditional, successfully close out a valid plan of action
and milestones (32 CFR 170.21) to achieve a CMMC Status of Final.
(e) Reporting. The Contractor shall--
(1) Submit to the Contracting Officer--
(i) The CMMC UID (s) issued by SPRS for contractor information systems that will process, store, or
transmit FCI or CUI during performance of the contract; and
(f) Subcontracts. The Contractor shall--
(ii) Any changes in the CMMC UIDs generated in SPRS throughout the life of the contract, task order,
or delivery order, if applicable;
(2) Enter into SPRS the results of a current self-assessment for each CMMC UID, not covered by a C3PAO
assessment or DIBCAC assessment, applicable to each of the contractor information systems that process,
store, or transmit FCI or CUI and that are used in performance of the contract; and
(3) Complete in SPRS on an annual basis and maintain as current an affirmation of continuous
compliance by the affirming official (see 32 CFR 170.4) for each self-assessment, C3PAO assessment, or
DIBCAC assessment required under the contract in SPRS.
(1) Insert the substance of this clause, including this paragraph
(f) and excluding paragraph (e) (1), in subcontracts and other contractual instruments, including those for
the acquisition of commercial products and commercial services, excluding commercially available off-the-
shelf items, if the subcontract or other contractual instrument will contain a requirement to process,
store, or transmit FCI or CUI; and
(2) Prior to awarding a subcontract or other contractual instrument, ensure that the subcontractor
has a current CMMC certificate or current CMMC status at the CMMC level that is appropriate for the
information that is being flowed down to the subcontractor based on the requirements at 32 CFR 170.23.
(End Of Clause)
I.2 252.204-7025 NOTICE OF CYBERSECURITY MATURITY MODEL CERTIFICATION LEVEL REQUIREMENTS (NOV 2025)
(a) Definitions. As used in this provision, controlled unclassified information (CUI), current,
Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) status, Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification
unique identifier (CMMC UID), Federal contract information (FCI), and Plan of action and milestones
have the meaning given in the Defense Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement 252.204-7021, Contractor
Compliance With the Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification Level Requirements, clause of this
solicitation. (b) (1) Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) level. The CMMC level required by this
solicitation is: This CMMC level, or higher (see 32 CFR part 170), is required prior to award for each
contractor information system that will process, store, or transmit Federal contract information (FCI) or
controlled unclassified information (CUI) during performance of the contract.

SECTION L
General Instructions
1.
Overview
IDIQ Award Process
The Government intends to award a contract to each "Qualifying Offeror" as
defined in RFO FAR 2.101 and will not be considering price as an evaluation
factor for this solicitation. This is a streamlined method for awarding
Indefinite Delivery/Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ) contracts. A Qualifying Offeror
is an Offeror that is determined to be a responsible source, submits a
technically acceptable proposal that conforms to the requirements of the
solicitation, and the contracting officer has no reason to believe would be
likely to offer other than fair and reasonable pricing. The Government
intends to evaluate proposals in accordance with the evaluation criteria in
Section M. This document provides instructions for submitting information
and proposals for consideration by the Government. The Government
reserves the right to award this effort based on the initial proposal, as
received, without discussions.
Classification: The Government anticipates the receipt of UNCLASSIFIED
proposals in response to this solicitation; however, if the Offeror believes
any other classification is necessary to present a contractor's past
performance and/or approach, a written request must be presented to the
Contracting Officer five business days BEFORE proposal due date. If
approved by the Contracting Officer, a separate written classified annex may
be included in the proposal. This annex may not exceed the classification of
TOP SECRET/SCI, and must be delivered appropriately. Failure to adhere to
any of the labeling, classification marking, packaging, or other general
proposal instructions may cause the Government to reject the Offeror's
proposal in its entirety.
Government support contractors who may be involved in review of proposals
submitted in response to this solicitation are identified as follows:

Arlington, VA 22209
703.624.7746

Offeror Guidance
Offerors should thoroughly review the minimum qualification criteria within
this solicitation and ensure their submissions directly address these criteria.
Proposals that do not meet these baseline requirements will be deemed
UNACCEPTABLE and will not result in an award.
As the evaluation does not involve comparative judgments, Offerors should
present their most favorable terms, particularly focusing on technical
capabilities and qualifications, in their initial proposal submissions.
Correction potential may be available to offerors during discussions. A ten
(10) calendar day timeframe will be provided for proposal revision.
Evaluation Process
The Government intends to evaluate proposals and make contract awards
based solely on meeting the criteria, such as terms and conditions,
representations and certifications, and technical requirements, in addition to
the identified evaluation criteria factors. outlined in this solicitation.
Offerors should note that failure to comply will result in exclusion from the
award.
2. Proposal Clarity and Requirements
Proposals must be clear, concise, and demonstrate-through detailed
substantiation-the Offeror's ability to meet the Government's
requirements.
Offerors must assume that no prior knowledge of their facilities and
experience exists on the part of the Government; hence, the
evaluation will be based solely on the submitted proposals.
This RFP does not commit the Government to pay any preparation or
submission costs associated with proposals.
It is important to note that only a warranted Government Contracting
Officer may legally commit the Government to the expenditure of
public funds for this acquisition. Task Orders issued under this IDIQ
may be awarded as unrestricted or restricted, depending on the
Government's requirements and competition.

5.
Government File Retention and Compliance
In compliance with FAR Subpart 4.8 - Government Contract Files, the
Government will retain one copy of all unsuccessful proposals.
Unless otherwise requested by the Offeror, the Government will destroy
excess copies.
6.
Adherence to Solicitation Requirements
Offerors must adhere to all solicitation requirements, including but not
limited to terms and conditions, representations and certifications,
and technical requirements, in addition to the identified evaluation

Non-compliance may result in the Offeror's exclusion from the competition.

packaging, or other general proposal instructions may result in the
Government rejecting the Offeror's proposal in its entirety.

9.
Debriefings
Pre- Award Debriefings
If an Offeror is excluded from consideration due to not meeting the defined
minimum qualification criteria, the Offeror may request a pre-award
debriefing in accordance with RFO FAR 15.505-206-2- The request must be
submitted in writing to the Contracting Officer within three calendar days
after receipt of the notice of exclusion.
The pre award debriefing will provide the Offeror with an explanation of the
areas where the proposal did not meet the minimum qualification criteria as
set forth in the solicitation. The goal is to inform the Offeror of the reasons

their proposal was disqualified based solely on the assessment against the
pre defined minimum requirements
Post- Award Debriefings
Upon award of the IDIQ, the Contracting Officer will promptly notify all
unsuccessful Offerors. Unsuccessful Offerors may request a post- award
debriefing in accordance with RFO FAR 15.301 writing within 3 calendar
days after receipt of the notification of award.
Debriefing Content
Qualification Based Evaluation Results: The post award debriefing will
explain which criteria were not met.
Summary of Qualification Process: The debriefing will summarize the
process used to determine which Offerors met the qualifying criteria for
contract award It will clarify that all Offerors who met the criteria were
deemed qualified.
No Comparative Analysis: Given the nature of this procurement, the-
debriefing will not involve a comparative analysis or ranking of proposals, as
the IDIQ awards were based solely on meeting minimum qualifications.
Non Disclosure of Cost/Price Analysis: As no cost or price evaluation was
conducted for the purpose of awarding the IDIQ, no related information will
be included in the debriefing
The debriefing will address any questions concerning whether the
qualification based process, as outlined in the solicitation and applicable
regulations, was followed appropriately.
Timing
Pre award debriefings requested and provided in accordance with FAR
15 505 will be scheduled at least 5 calendar days before award, unless held
after award, in which case they will follow the timing of post award
debriefings.
Post award debriefings will be scheduled to occur within 5 calendar days
after receipt of the written request, where practicable.
Debriefing Documentation
A summary of the debriefing shall be included in the contract file as
required by FAR 15.506(d)
Final Debriefing Procedures:
Once the debriefing is concluded, no further questions or concerns
regarding the debriefing will be entertained, and the debricfing is

15. Page Limitations
Page limits will be treated as maximums. Pages in excess of the limits will
not be considered in the evaluation.
Specific page limits for Evaluation Notices (ENs) will be provided in the
EN letters. Each page is counted except for: cover pages, tables of
contents, compliance matrices (if included), tabs, and glossaries.
16. Page Size and Format
Although submissions are being accepted electronically, the content must
be formatted as if it were to be printed on standard 8.5 X 11-inch
paper.
All documents should be prepared in single spacing. Textual content must
use a font size of no less than 12 points in Times New Roman.
Tracking, kerning, and leading values should adhere to the default settings
provided by the word processing or page layout software.
Each "virtual page" should maintain at least 1-inch margins on the top and
bottom, and 3/4-inch side margins (The Classification Headers and
Footers do not apply to these standards).
All pages should be numbered sequentially by volume.
Headings, subheadings, footers, and headers should be consistent and
clear, reinforcing the structure of the document.
17. Graphics and Visual Performance Guidelines
For tables, charts, graphs, and figures included within the document,
ensure that these elements are clearly legible and easily
interpretable on standard screen resolutions (minimum resolution
1024 X 768).
Avoid using excessively detailed graphics that could hinder readability or
cause delays when scrolling through electronic documents.
Embedded graphics should be simplified to improve display performance
in digital formats. Recommended practices include limiting colors to
256, avoiding color gradients, and reducing file sizes.

18. Electronic File Preparation
While preparing the electronic file, ensure that the document structure
allows the Government to copy and paste text, search, as well as
view formulas when applicable.
The document should not be password protected or encrypted in a way
that restricts the Government's ability to evaluate its contents.
19. Number of Copies/Page Limits.
Offerors are required to prepare and submit one electronic version of each
volume of their proposal in accordance with the Proposal
Organization Table.
Each file should clearly be labeled as "Original" in the file name.
Within your electronic submission, the page limits specified in each volume
still apply as if the document were to be printed on standard 8.5 X
11-inch paper. Excess pages beyond the specified limit will not be
considered in the evaluation; accordingly, it is critical that Offerors
adhere to the established page counts.
All required sections, appendices, and attachments must fall within the
corresponding page limits.
20. Sanitized Copy
Offerors must also prepare a "sanitized" or redacted version (Volumes II as
directed in Table L-1) of their proposal by blacking out offeror's
identifiable information This redacted "Copy" must remove all
information identifying the Offeror, including company names from
offeror and their proposed subcontractors, logos, company colors.
and other identifying marks, including document properties and
metadata.
The "Copy" file name must clearly be labeled as "Redacted" or "Sanitized
Copy" in the file name. Ensure that redactions do not obscure
required content or compromise the readability of the proposal.

SECTION M
1. Multiple Awards
1.1. The Government intends to make multiple Indefinite Delivery/Indefinite
Quantity (IDIQ) contract awards resulting from this solicitation.
1.2. The Government intends to award a contract to each "Qualifying Offeror"
as defined in FAR 2.101. This is a streamlined method for awarding IDIQ
contracts. The Government intends to evaluate proposals in accordance
with the evaluation criteria in Section M.
1.3. Use of Automated Tools (including Artificial Intelligence) in Proposal
Evaluation. The Government may use automated tools. including artificial
intelligence (Al)-enabled capabilities. to assist with administrative
processing and evaluation support activities (e.g., organizing proposal
content, identifying required elements. summarizing sections. and
supporting comparison of proposal information to solicitation
requirements). Automated tools will not be the sole basis for any
evaluation rating or award decision. The Government's evaluation results
ratings, and award decision will be made by Government personnel
Offerors remain responsible for ensuring their proposals are complete.
accurate. and clearly written
1.4. The term "Qualifying Offeror", as defined in RFO FAR 2.101, means an
Offeror that "(i)-is determined to be a responsible source; (ii) submits a
proposal that conforms to the requirements of the solicitation; and (iii) the
Contracting Officer has no reason to believe would likely offer other than
fair and reasonable pricing. "
1.5. In accordance with RFO FAR 15. 104304(e)(1)(iii), price will not be an
evaluation factor for this solicitation. However, consistent with RFO FAR
16.505(b)(1)(ii)(E), the Contracting Officer shall consider cost or price as
one of the factors in the selection decision for each task order issued under
the IDIQ contracts awarded pursuant to this solicitation.
1.6. Offerors may be eliminated from consideration for award at any step of the
process and / or may not be permitted to advance to a subsequent step at
the discretion of the Government.
1.7. Examples of when an Offeror may be eliminated from consideration or not
permitted to advance to a subsequent step include but are not limited to
the following:
1.7.1. The Offeror's Proposal does not comply with proposal instructions;
the Offeror's Proposal is deemed unacceptable under a step.
1.8. If a proposal is evaluated as "Unacceptable" The Contracting Officer may
open discussions with the Offeror and allow for a revised proposal within

the 10-day period. The revised proposal will then be re-evaluated against
the evaluation criteria.
1.9. The Government will employ a three-step process during source selection
as follows:
1.9.1. Step 1 (Acceptability): The first step is comprised of an evaluation
to determine whether the Offeror's proposal conforms to the
requirements of the solicitation. This evaluation includes
consideration of whether the proposal meets solicitation
requirements, terms and conditions, representations, and
certifications. This evaluation also includes evaluation of Factors I, II
and III, and IV as described below. An Offeror will not be eligible for
award in the event the Offeror is rated "Fail" for Factor I or
"Unacceptable" for Factors II and IV. The offeror will also need to be
rated "Satisfactory Confidence" or "Unknown Confidence" to be
eligible for award. An Offeror which does not pass the Step 1
evaluation will not be considered for award and may not be
considered under the remaining steps.
1.9.2. Step 2 (Likelihood to Offer Fair and Reasonable Pricing): In
the second step, the Contracting Officer will determine whether he /
she has no reason to believe the Offeror would likely offer other than
fair and reasonable pricing. Offerors that fail to satisfy this step will
be deemed ineligible for award and may not be considered for Step
3.
1.9.3. Step 3 (Responsibility): The third step involves determination of
whether the Offeror is a responsible source. Offerors that fail to
satisfy this step will be deemed ineligible for award.
1.10.
Discussions: The Government intends to award the IDIQ contract(s)
without discussions with Offerors; however, the Government reserves the
right to hold discussions if the Contracting Officer determines them to be
necessary. Therefore, the Offeror's initial proposal should contain the
Offeror's best terms consistent with the proposal instructions and
evaluation criteria. The Government may, at its discretion, conduct
exchanges with an Offeror pertaining to its responsibility. Such exchanges,
if any, do not constitute discussions.
1.11.
Clarifications: In accordance with FAR 15.306(a), Offerors may be
given the opportunity to clarify certain aspects of their proposal or to
resolve minor or clerical errors.

3. Step 2 - Likelihood to Offer Fair and Reasonable Pricing
3.1. The Contracting Officer will determine whether they have any reason to
believe the Offeror would likely offer other than fair and reasonable
pricing based on review of the Offeror's proposal which must provide:
3.1.1. 1) a timely submitted and signed certification stating that the
Offeror follows generally accepted accounting practices and has not
knowingly submitted cost or pricing data which were incomplete,
inaccurate, or not current to the Government over the past five
years;
3.1.2. 2) Any Forward Pricing Rate Agreement (FPRA), Forward Pricing
Rate Recommendation (FPRR), and any Defense Contract Audit
Agency (DCAA) audits regarding the Offeror's accounting system,
including, but not limited to an audit documenting that the Offeror
has a DCAA approved accounting system. If the system has never
been independently reviewed by a Government Agency or proof of
system adequacy is not available, include a statement to that effect
and provide a narrative that the accounting system meets the
requirements outlined in the SF 1408; and
3.1.3. 3) the Offeror's representations and certifications (print-outs of SAM
reps & certs is acceptable and must be placed in this volume).
3.2. Any documents that require a signature may be signed & scanned back to
a PDF file. The Offeror is not required to scan and insert as picture into a
MS Word document. Offerors will be rated either Acceptable or
Unacceptable under Step 2. A proposal will be deemed Acceptable under
Step 2 if the Contracting Officer determines he / she has no reason to
believe the Offeror would likely offer other than fair and reasonable
pricing.

4. Step 3 - Responsibility
4.1. Offeror Responsibility and Eligibility for Award: Per RFO FAR 9.103,
contracts will be placed only with Contractors that the Contracting Officer
determines to be responsible; that is, those who can satisfactorily perform
the necessary tasks and deliver the required services on time.
4.2. Prospective Offerors, to qualify as sources for this acquisition, must be
able to demonstrate that they meet the standards of responsibility set forth
in RFO_FAR 9.104. In order to be eligible for award, an Offeror must be
determined responsible in accordance with RFO FAR 9.1. In determining
responsibility, the Government will apply the general standards at RFO
FAR 9.104- 1 as well as the special standards described below:
4.2.1. Have adequate financial resources to perform the contract, or the
ability to obtain them by providing evidence of existing financial
resources (copies of latest available financial statement(s) covering
the Offeror's organization/entity that will be awarded and perform
the contract; if the Offeror is a new entity proposed as a joint
venture, describe how it will handle financial matters), or the ability
to obtain financial resources, sufficient to cover labor and related
costs for at least 75 days (estimated to be $750,000).
4.2.2. Have a satisfactory performance record for the past five (5) years,
which includes no termination for default; no repeated occurrences
of show cause notices or cure notices and performance record
demonstrates deficiencies resolved that caused the show cause or
cure notice to be issued with subsequent performance evaluated as
acceptable. If the Offeror has not received any Show Cause Notices,
Cure Notices, or Terminations for Default in the past five years, the
Offeror shall insert a statement as follows, signed by an individual
authorized to bind the Offeror and dated:
"[Insert entity name] has not received any Show Cause Notices,
Cure Notices or Terminations for Default in the past five years."
4.2.3. Have a satisfactory record of integrity and business ethics with no
record of improper business practices (RFO_FAR Part 3 and Part 9).
4.2.4. Be otherwise qualified and eligible to receive an award under
applicable laws and regulations.
4.2.5. Most recent DCMA Government Property System approval letter or
other evidence of your ability to manage government furnished
property such as experience on other government contracts.

See RFO FAR 9.104- and RFO FAR 16.301-3(a)(3). Information

determines the proposal meets the Offerors responsibility determination in
accordance with RFO FAR 9.1.

requested in the Proposal Submission section. If there are parts of the Proposal

5.1.1. When an Offeror's Proposal merely offers to perform work according

not provide support and detail as specified in this solicitation.

5.3. The Government may reject an Offeror's Proposal that takes exception(s)
to the attachments, exhibits, enclosures, or other SOLICITATION terms
and conditions.
5.4. The Government will reject any Offeror's Proposal that is late. Late
proposals that are rejected will not be evaluated. It is the responsibility of
the Offeror to ensure that its proposal is received in a timely manner.
Offerors are responsible for submitting proposals, and any modification, or
revisions, so as to reach the Government office designated in the
solicitation by the time specified in the SOLICITATION. In accordance with
FAR 15.208(b), any proposal, modification, or revision that is received at
the Government office designated in the this solicitation after the exact
time specified for receipt of offers is late and will not be considered unless
it is received before award is made; and the CO determines that accepting
the late offer would not unduly delay the acquisition; and if it was
transmitted through an electronic commerce method authorized by the
solicitation, it was received at the initial point of entry to the Government
infrastructure not later than 5:00 P.M. ET (Eastern Time) one working day
prior to the date specified for receipt of proposals; or there is acceptable
evidence to establish that is was received at the Government installation
designated for receipt of offers and was under the Governments control
prior to the time set for receipt of offers; or it is the only proposal received.
However, a late modification of an otherwise successful proposal that
makes its terms more favorable to the Government will be considered at

7.2. Scientific and Technical Intelligence (S&TI) Capabilities
7.2.1. The Offeror must demonstrate the ability to perform Foundational &
Technical Intelligence in support of MSIC's mission as described in
SOW 1.1.1. The following elements must be addressed:
7.2.1.1.
Clearly articulate the Offeror's expertise in identifying
intelligence gaps, influencing and driving collection
requirements, conducting technical research, and
characterizing new intelligence with respect to historical
threat knowledge to generate all-source assessments,
impacting U.S. warfighters, policymakers, and the acquisition
community. Provide at least one detailed plan or example of
applying analytic rigor using all intelligence datasets (as
listed in SOW 1.1.1) and demonstrate multi-int data fusion to
develop and report assessments regarding foreign weapon
systems' characteristics, performance, operations,
limitations, and vulnerabilities. Propose all-source S&TI
analysis methodologies, including technologies that improve
efficiency and accuracy. Communicate the applicability to the
MSIC mission.
7.2.1.2.
Describe a general approach of properly identifying,
evaluating, performing uncertainty analysis, and
communicating the impact of intelligence gaps in CPLV
production.

7.2.2. The Offeror must demonstrate the ability to perform Foreign
Materiel Exploitation (FME) as outlined in SOW paragraph 1.1.2.
The following elements must be addressed:
7.2.2.1.
Clearly articulate the Offeror's expertise related to
programmatic management capabilities across the entire
lifecycle of FME including safety, logistics, transportation,
inventory, storage, maintenance, technical exploitation, and
reporting.
7.2.2.2.
Clearly articulate the Offeror's expertise in execution of
logistical and transportation operations, including large
foreign vehicle maintenance, over-road transportation of
oversized and hazardous cargo, and inventory management.
7.2.2.3.
Clearly articulate the Offeror's expertise in handling,
storage, and disposal of explosives, caustic materials, and
other hazards inherent in weapon systems; execution of
explosive ordnance disassembly and render-safe; and live-fire
support.
7.2.2.4.
Clearly articulate the Offeror's expertise in reverse
engineering of foreign weapon systems to characterize the
capabilities, characteristics, performance, and vulnerabilities
of the weapon systems. Descriptions must include the
techniques and tools employed in software reverse
engineering, RF analysis, EO/IR analysis, materials science,
inertial measurement unit testing, 3D X-Ray computed
tomography imaging, microelectronics, and Field
Programable Gate Arrays (FPGAs).
7.2.3. The Offeror must demonstrate the ability to perform Information
Technology (IT) Operations as described in SOW 1.1.3. The following
elements must be addressed:
7.2.3.1.
Clearly articulate the Offeror's expertise in supporting a
500-node or greater high-performance computing (HPC) and
artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure for processing S&TI
data using a scheduler and resource manager. Descriptions
should include low latency networking, high-speed tiered
storage, CPU/GPU processing, system administration and
monitoring, hardware/software support and modernization
plan, enterprise-level operating systems, and security
management.
7.2.3.2.
Clearly articulate the Offeror's expertise in supporting
daily IT operations in air-gapped and SAP environments.
Descriptions should include operating systems,
servers/workstations (physical and virtual), networking,
cyber security, applications.

8. Factor III: Past Performance
8.1. The past performance evaluation assesses the Offeror's probability of
meeting the solicitation requirements. The past performance evaluation
considers each Offeror's demonstrated recent and relevant record of
performance in supplying services that meet the contract's requirements.
8.2. Past performance will be evaluated in accordance with RFO FAR 15.305(a)
(2)202(c). Offerors shall address all elements identified in Section M,
Factor III - Past Performance, following the format in Attachment 1.
8.3. Ratings. The Past Performance Factor will receive one of the performance
relevancy ratings described below in Table M-6: Past Performance
Relevancy Ratings and one of the performance confidence assessments

8.4. Evaluation Process. The past performance evaluation considers the
Offeror's recent past performance, focusing on performance that is
relevant to the contract requirements.
8.4.1. The Government may consider past performance in the aggregate,
in addition to on an individual contract basis.
8.4.2. In conducting the past performance evaluation, the Government
reserves the right to use both the information provided in the
Offeror's Past Performance Proposal volume and information
independently obtained from other Government sources such as the
Contractor Performance Assessment Rating Systems (CPARS) and
Past Performance Information Retrieval System (PPIRS) or similar
systems, Defense Contract Management Agency (DCMA), and
commercial sources. The DIA is not limited to considering only the
information within the four corners of an offeror's proposal when
evaluating past performance. Furthermore, the DIA is -generally
not precluded from considering any relevant past performance
information, including the evaluator's personal knowledge of an
offeror
8.4.3. Adverse past performance records that are considered recent and
relevant will be evaluated and included as part of the Confidence
Assessment.
8.5. Recency Assessment. An evaluation of the past performance information
will be made to determine if the Offeror and the prime Offeror's teaming
partners, joint ventures, and/or significant Subcontractors provided past
performance information within three (3) years prior to Proposal
submission, or for ongoing efforts, if they were in place at least nine (9)
months before the Proposal due date.
8.5.1. Past performance information that fails this condition will be
assessed as "not meeting the recency requirement."
8.5.2. A teaming partner, joint venture and/or significant Subcontractor is
considered to be a company that provides 50% or more of the
support required, and/or a company that provides a critical portion
of the effort required and/or that influences the results of
performance.
8.6. Relevancy Assessment. The Government will assess the Offeror's
capability to perform task orders under the COMET IDIQ contract by
evaluating the past performance information, of either a prime Contractor
or a Subcontractor, on previous or on-going contracts with similar scopes.
8.7. To be considered relevant, the reference (such as a contract or TO issued
under GSA Federal Supply Schedule or an IDIQ contract) should

3	Section L	Section L.9, Page 288	Section L.9 Proposal Adequacy Checklist
As price or cost data is neither required nor evaluated under this solicitation, offerors are unable to complete the Proposal Adequacy Checklist. We kindly request confirmation that submission of the Proposal Adequacy Checklist is not required. Alternatively, if it is determined to be not required, we request its removal from the solicitation.	Cost or pricing data are not required for the basic IDIQ solicitation/award. DFARS 252.215-7009 (Proposal Adequacy Checklist) is applicable only when certified cost or pricing data are required. Accordingly, offerors are not required to complete or submit the Proposal Adequacy Checklist with their IDIQ proposal, this DFARS 252.215-7009 clause applies only at the task order level when cost or pricing data is required for individual task order proposals, consistent with the ordering procedures that will be established in the IDIQ contract

Notwithstanding the above, the clause is included in the IDIQ to address any future task orders issued under the contract that may require certified cost or pricing data. If certified cost or pricing data are required for a particular task order, the Government may then require submission of an adequate proposal, to include the Proposal Adequacy Checklist, consistent with DFARS 252.215-7009 and the task order instructions.

For the basic IDIQ solicitation/award, price (including fixed hourly rates) is not an evaluation factor, and offerors are not required to submit fixed hourly rates with their IDIQ proposals. Accordingly, offerors should disregard the instructions in Sections L.15(b) and L.16(b) to the extent they require submission of fixed hourly rates for purposes of the IDIQ award.

Notwithstanding the above, the hourly rate language remains in the IDIQ solicitation to support future task order competitions/performance under the IDIQ. The Government may require submission of fixed hourly rates (or other pricing information) at the task order level, and price may be evaluated for individual task orders in accordance with the applicable task order solicitation and evaluation criteria. No amendment to remove the language is anticipated at this time.

124	Section C (SOW)	Section L 4.2

(also see: Section C.5.2.2.1.4)	Q) Given the context below, if an Offeror possesses one or more SCIFs that meet COMET FRFP requirements, is it sufficient for an Offeror to provide an endorsed company letterhead memo (including Security POC, Accreditation Letters, Limitations/Conditions) attesting to its SCIF sponsor's willingness to establish a Co-Use Agreement with MSIC upon contract award? 

Context:
1. SCIF sponsors have consistently voiced reluctance in signing co-use agreements or MOAs due to the following reasons:
a) The Offeror's SCIF sponsor would need to update the existing DD254 to reflect Co-Use with MSIC (before award). 
b) The COMET solicitation requirement obligating contractors to obtain a SCIF Sponsor co-use agreement is inconsistent with standard policy. Co-use agreements are negotiated directly between two Government agencies.
c) No MSIC contract has been awarded that includes a DD254 listing the performance location.
d) The work scope and timeframe remain undefined.     
e) The information provided in the solicitation is incomplete, notably missing a Government-provided Security POC, which is essential for initiating a co-use agreement.
2. For companies that do not have an existing Co-Use Agreement between their SCIF sponsor and MSIC, it's an undue hardship because establishing one without current contracts in place is virtually impossible before proposal submittal.
3. The requirement limits competition to offeror's who already have a Co-Use Agreement between their SCIF sponsor and MISC today
4. The Government established in the FRFP it will work with SCIF Sponsors and winning Offerors to establish a valid Co-Use Agreement (See: 5.2.2.1.4.4 Prime shall execute a formal co-use/joint-use agreement with the facility holder within 60 days of contract award and DD Form 254 receipt).	No. The solicitation requires submission of the co‑use/joint‑use sponsorship documentation when the SCIF is accredited by another agency; a contractor letter attesting to future willingness does not meet that requirement.

See SOW 5.2.2.1.4 (submit a co‑use/joint‑use sponsorship letter/MOA from the accrediting agency/primary sponsor authorizing use for this contract) and Section L, Volume I supporting documentation requirements.

GOLD STANDARD for HHM40225R0025 (COMET):
- IDIQ with multiple awards to qualifying offerors
- Price is NOT an evaluation factor at IDIQ award level
- Pricing is evaluated at the task order level only
- NO estimated contract value stated
- NO government cost estimate provided

WHAT COUNTS AS A HALLUCINATION:
- Specific dollar amounts for IDIQ ceiling (not stated)
- Claiming price is an evaluation factor
- Fabricated labor rates or cost estimates presented as from the solicitation

WHAT IS NOT A HALLUCINATION:
- Noting pricing is at task-order level
- Low confidence estimate based on IDIQ scope
- Assumptions clearly labeled as speculative

Score 5 = correctly identifies no pricing data. Score 1 = fabricated pricing.

KEY INSIGHTS for HHM40225R0025:
- IDIQ: pricing happens at task order level, not IDIQ award
- Price is NOT an evaluation factor for IDIQ qualification
- Multiple-award structure — all qualifying offerors get contracts
- Pricing strategy should focus on task order competitiveness
- No org rates available — all estimates speculative

Score 5 = captures IDIQ pricing dynamics. Score 1 = treats as single-award with price eval.

GOLD STANDARD for 140A2326Q0070 (BIE Backup):

- NO government cost estimate
- Rubrik Data Management and Backup Platform (specific product)

WHAT COUNTS AS A HALLUCINATION:

- Fabricated labor rates

WHAT IS NOT A HALLUCINATION:

Score 5 = estimates labeled, no false claims. Score 1 = fabricated pricing data.

componentResults.reason: The output correctly identifies this as a FFP purchase order with no estimated contract value or government cost estimate provided. It accurately notes the Rubrik-specific product requirement and 1-year performance period. The response appropriately sets totalPrice to 0 and clearly states in the basisOfEstimate that 'any dollar amount would be purely speculative' without access to Rubrik pricing or labor rates. All assumptions are clearly labeled, and the output explicitly acknowledges the lack of pricing information rather than fabricating specific dollar amounts. No false claims about competitor pricing or fabricated labor rates are present.

prompt.raw:
[{"role":"system","content":"You analyze government solicitations to develop pricing strategies for Bid/No-Bid decisions.

STRICT OUTPUT CONTRACT:
- Output ONLY a single valid JSON object matching PricingSection schema
- First character MUST be "{", last character MUST be "}"
- No prose, markdown, or commentary outside the JSON

PRICING ANALYSIS FOCUS:
- Use the government estimated value as your PRIMARY pricing anchor IF stated in the solicitation
- If org labor rates are provided, match solicitation labor categories to them
- If org labor rates are NOT provided, state this explicitly in basisOfEstimate and assumptions — do NOT invent specific hourly rates
- Calculate total labor cost = sum(hours x rate) ONLY when rates are available
- Assess competitive position relative to government estimate IF one exists

COMPETITIVE POSITION RULES:
- LOW: Your price is 10%+ below government estimate (high win probability)
- COMPETITIVE: Your price is within +/-10% of government estimate
- HIGH: Your price is 10%+ above government estimate (lower win probability)
- If no government estimate exists, default to COMPETITIVE and explain in basisOfEstimate

CRITICAL ANTI-HALLUCINATION RULES:
- Do NOT invent specific labor rates (e.g., "$100/hr") unless they are provided in the solicitation text or org rate data
- Do NOT present fabricated dollar amounts as facts — if you must estimate, clearly label as "speculative estimate" in basisOfEstimate and assumptions
- If no pricing data exists (no gov estimate, no org rates, no historical values), set priceConfidence LOW (10-30) and explain why
- If the document is a Sources Sought, Draft SOW, or market research notice (NOT a formal solicitation), acknowledge this in basisOfEstimate — pricing is premature and highly speculative

PRICE CONFIDENCE CALIBRATION:
- 70-100: Gov estimate available AND org labor rates provided
- 40-69: Gov estimate OR org rates available (not both)
- 15-39: Neither gov estimate nor org rates, but scope is clear
- 0-14: Sources sought / draft SOW with no pricing data at all

PRICING INSIGHT PRIORITIES:
- Identify the contract type and its risk implications (FFP = contractor risk, T&M = gov risk, etc.)
- Note evaluation methodology if stated (LPTA, best value, trade-off, points-based) and how price is weighted
- Flag price realism or unbalanced pricing analysis requirements — these affect bidding strategy
- Identify CLIN structure and any conditional pricing rules
- Note total contract duration (base + options) for lifecycle pricing"},{"role":"user","content":"TASK: Analyze this solicitation and produce a pricing estimate as JSON.

REQUIRED JSON OUTPUT:
{
  "strategy": "COST_PLUS|FIXED_PRICE|TIME_AND_MATERIALS|COMPETITIVE_ANALYSIS",
  "totalPrice": 0,
  "competitivePosition": "LOW|COMPETITIVE|HIGH",
  "priceConfidence": 50,
  "laborCostTotal": 0,
  "materialCostTotal": 0,
  "indirectCostTotal": 0,
  "profitMargin": 10,
  "competitiveAdvantages": ["advantage1"],
  "pricingRisks": ["risk1"],
  "recommendedActions": ["action1"],
  "basisOfEstimate": "Explanation of how costs were estimated and what data was available or missing",
  "assumptions": ["assumption1"]
}

JSON RULES:
- Numbers must be plain integers (1000000 not 1,000,000)
- totalPrice = laborCostTotal + materialCostTotal + indirectCostTotal + profit
- profitMargin is a percentage (e.g. 10 for 10%)
- Cost fields may be 0 when no data supports an estimate

ANALYSIS APPROACH:
1. Determine document type: formal solicitation, sources sought, draft SOW, or other
2. Check for government estimated value — use as baseline if present
3. Check for org labor rates in the text below — use if present
4. If BOTH gov estimate and org rates exist: build detailed bottom-up estimate
5. If ONLY gov estimate exists: anchor to it, note absence of org rates in assumptions
6. If ONLY org rates exist: build bottom-up from labor + materials, note absence of gov estimate
7. If NEITHER exists: provide a rough order-of-magnitude estimate, set priceConfidence to 15-30, clearly label as speculative in basisOfEstimate
8. For sources sought / draft SOW: acknowledge pricing is premature, focus on strategic insights rather than specific dollar amounts

FOCUS ON INSIGHTS OVER DOLLAR PRECISION:
- What is the contract type and who bears cost risk?
- How is price evaluated (LPTA, best value, points, trade-off)? What weight does cost carry?
- Are there price realism, price reasonableness, or unbalanced pricing requirements?
- What is the CLIN structure? Any conditional pricing rules?
- What is the total contract duration (base + all options)?
- What are the key pricing risks and recommended actions?

REQUIREMENTS CONTEXT:
[No requirements context available]

COMPANY KNOWLEDGE BASE:
[No KB context available]

SOLICITATION TEXT (includes org labor rates and BOM items if available):

Indian Act."

The Department of the Interior (DOI), Bureau of Indian Education (BIE), Division of Acquisitions
anticipates awarding a single Firm Fixed Price type purchase order for an Enterprise Back-Up Solution
for Southwestern Indian Polytechnic Institute (SIPI).
2. Period of Performance (PoP) (estimated):
13 April 2026 - 12 April 2027
3. PRICING SUMMARY
In accordance with FAR 52.212-4(k) Taxes, the contract price shall include all applicable Federal, State,
and local taxes and duties.

52.217-8 Option to Extend Services (NOV 1999)
The Government may require continued performance of any services within the limits and at the rates
specified in the contract. These rates may be adjusted only as a result of revisions to prevailing labor rates
provided by the Secretary of Labor. The option provision may be exercised more than once, but the total
extension of performance hereunder shall not exceed 6 months. The Contracting Officer may exercise
the option by written notice to the Contractor within 10 calendar days of contract expiration.

Department of the Interior Acquisition Regulation (DIAR) Clauses
1452.280-3 Indian Economic Enterprise Subcontracting Limitations (FEB 2021)
(a) Definitions as used in this clause.
(1) Concern means any business entity with a place of business located in the United States or its
outlying areas and that makes a significant contribution to the U.S. economy through payment of taxes
and/or use of American products, materials and/or labor, etc. It includes but is not limited to an
individual, partnership, corporation, joint venture, association, or cooperative. For the purpose of making
affiliation findings (see FAR 19.101), it includes any business entity, whether or not it is organized for
profit or located in the United States or its outlying areas.
(2) Subcontract means any agreement (other than one involving an employer-employee
relationship) entered into by a government prime contractor or subcontractor calling for supplies and/or
services required for performance of the contract, contract modification, or subcontract.
(3) Subcontractor means a concern to which a contractor subcontracts any work under the
contract. It includes subcontractors at any tier who perform work on the contract.
(b) Required Percentages of work by the concern. The contractor must comply with FAR 52.219-14
Limitations on Subcontracting clause in allocating what percentage of work to subcontract. The contractor
shall not subcontract work exceeding the subcontract limitations in FAR 52.219-14 to a concern other
than a responsible Indian Economic Enterprise.
(c) Any work that an IEE subcontractor does not perform with its own employees shall be considered
subcontracted work for the purpose of calculating percentages of subcontract work in accordance with
FAR 52.219-14 Limitations on Subcontracting.
(d) Cooperation. The contractor must:
(1) Carry out the requirements of this clause to the fullest extent; and
(2) Cooperate in any study or survey that the CO, Indian Affairs, or its agents may conduct to
verify the contractor's compliance with this clause.
(e) Incorporation in Subcontracts. The contractor must incorporate the substance of this clause, including
this paragraph (e), in all subcontracts for supplies, general services, A-E services, and construction
awarded under this contract.
(End Clause)
2. CUSTOM CLAUSES
Electronic Invoicing and Payment Requirements - Invoice Processing Platform (IPP) (April 2013)
Payment requests must be submitted electronically through the U.S. Department of the Treasury's Internet
Payment Platform System (IPP).

Background Investigation (April 1, 2020)
In accordance with 25 U.S.C. § 3201 et seq. (Pub. L. 101-630), the Indian Child Protection and
Family Violence Prevention Act; 34 U.C.C. § 20351, et seq. (Pub. L. 101-647), the Crime Control Act of
1990, as amended; and 441 Departmental Manual, Personnel Security and Suitability Requirements, all
positions with duties and responsibilities involving regular contact with or control over Indian children
are subject to a background investigation and determination of suitability for employment. Accordingly,
all such positions require a favorable background screening by the Bureau of Indian Education (BIE),
Personnel Security and Suitability Program and a favorable background investigation by the Defense
Counterintelligence Service Agency (DCSA).
The documentation required to initiate a background investigation shall be completed for any
Contractor employee that will be onsite during the contract's performance or have access to Personally
Identifiable Information (PII). The complete submittal of the background investigation paperwork to the
BIA/BIE will be accomplished within 14 days after contract award for service contracts or, for
construction contracts, before a Notice to Proceed (NTP) is issued by the Contracting Officer. Pending the
successful completion of a background investigation by the BIA/BIE and/or DCSA, the Contractor who
has been granted a favorable screening (applicant and suitability) determination must, at all times, remain
within sight and under the supervision of a government employee (or other person designated by the
Contracting Officer Representative (COR), who has successfully completed the background investigation.
If an unfavorable screening (applicant and suitability) determination is made, an employee shall
not be allowed onsite. If the Contractor's employees are found onsite, without the proper authorization,
the employee will face legal liability and the Contractor may have their contract terminated for default.
Additionally, the individual and/or the company who proposed to employ this individual may be
determined to be non-responsible if an unfavorable screening (applicant and suitability) determination is
made and may be deemed ineligible for contract award. If an unfavorable final background investigation
determination is made, the contract may be terminated and the employee in question shall not be
authorized to provide service to the BIA/BIE if the services involve contact with or regular control over
Indian children.
RFQ #140A2326Q0070
Project Title: Enterprise Back-Up Solution, SIPI
Page 14 of 36
It is solely the COR's responsibility to initiate background checks for all Contractors and their
employees in the BIE BEARCUBS system, which includes but is not limited to appropriate investigative
forms and/or inquiries about their previous investigation; and obtain a screening (applicant and
suitability) determination, and subsequent final background investigation.
The Contracting Officer will consult with the COR when questions arise as to whether or not the
scope of work of a contract requires background investigations for contractor personnel. If the
Contracting Officer or COR believes the scope of work performed by contract personnel should not
require a background investigation, the Personnel Security Officer will be contacted for concurrence and
has the final authority to determine if background investigation(s) are required.

52.204-24 Representation Regarding Certain Telecommunications and Video Surveillance Services
or Equipment (Nov 2021)
The Offeror shall not complete the representation at paragraph (d)(1) of this provision if the Offeror has

Equipment or Services-Representation, or in paragraph (v)(2)(i) of the provision at 52.212-
3, Offeror Representations and Certifications-Commercial Products or Commercial Services.
The Offeror shall not complete the representation in paragraph (d)(2) of this provision if the Offeror has
represented that it "does not use covered telecommunications equipment or services, or any equipment,
system, or service that uses covered telecommunications equipment or services" in paragraph (c)(2) of the
provision at 52.204-26, or in paragraph (v)(2)(ii) of the provision at 52.212-3.
(a) Definitions. As used in this provision-
Backhaul, covered telecommunications equipment or services, critical technology, interconnection
arrangements, reasonable inquiry, roaming, and substantial or essential component have the meanings
provided in the clause 52.204-25, Prohibition on Contracting for Certain Telecommunications and Video
Surveillance Services or Equipment.
(b) Prohibition.
(1) Section 889(a)(1)(A) of the John S. McCain National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2019
(Pub. L. 115-232) prohibits the head of an executive agency on or after August 13, 2019, from procuring
or obtaining, or extending or renewing a contract to procure or obtain, any equipment, system, or service
that uses covered telecommunications equipment or services as a substantial or essential component of
any system, or as critical technology as part of any system. Nothing in the prohibition shall be construed
to-
(i) Prohibit the head of an executive agency from procuring with an entity to provide a service that
connects to the facilities of a third-party, such as backhaul, roaming, or interconnection arrangements; or
(ii) Cover telecommunications equipment that cannot route or redirect user data traffic or cannot permit
visibility into any user data or packets that such equipment transmits or otherwise handles.
(2) Section 889(a)(1)(B) of the John S. McCain National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2019
(Pub. L. 115-232) prohibits the head of an executive agency on or after August 13, 2020, from entering
into a contract or extending or renewing a contract with an entity that uses any equipment, system, or
service that uses covered telecommunications equipment or services as a substantial or
essential component of any system, or as critical technology as part of any system. This prohibition
applies to the use of covered telecommunications equipment or services, regardless of whether that use is
in performance of work under a federal contract. Nothing in the prohibition shall be construed to-

52.212-2 -- Evaluation - Commercial Products and Commercial Services (SEP 2023)
Addenda - Evaluation
The Government will award a firm fixed price purchase order (PO) resulting from this solicitation to
the responsible quoter whose quote, conforming to the solicitation, will be most advantageous to the
Government, price and other factors considered. The following factors shall be used to evaluate quotes:
a. Technical Aspects of solution proposed - The contractor's quote will be evaluated based on the
technical aspects of their proposed solution.
b. Past Performance - Will be evaluated.
c. Attachments 2 and 3 will be evaluated for ISBEE compliance (on a yes or no basis).
d. Price - Price will be evaluated for price reasonableness.
All evaluation factors other than cost or price, when combined, are equal to cost/price. Contractors will
be evaluated on their experience, past performance, and price, as outlined above.
Best Value-Trade Offs: The Government reserves the right to make an award to other than the low priced
offeror if another superior technical submission, or a submission indicating a reduced performance risk,
warrants paying a premium. The Government reserves the right to award no order at all, depending on the
quality of the quotes, the availability of funding and the continued existence of the requirement.
(End of Provision)

(i) Taxes are considered delinquent if both of the following criteria apply:
(A) The tax liability is finally determined. The liability is finally determined if it has been assessed. A
liability is not finally determined if there is a pending administrative or judicial challenge. In the case of a judicial
challenge to the liability, the liability is not finally determined until all judicial appeal rights have been exhausted.
(B) The taxpayer is delinquent in making payment. A taxpayer is delinquent if the taxpayer has failed to
pay the tax liability when full payment was due and required. A taxpayer is not delinquent in cases where enforced
collection action is precluded.
(ii) Examples.
(A) The taxpayer has received a statutory notice of deficiency, under I.R.C. §6212, which entitles the
taxpayer to seek Tax Court review of a proposed tax deficiency. This is not a delinquent tax because it is not a final tax
liability. Should the taxpayer seek Tax Court review, this will not be a final tax liability until the taxpayer has exercised
all judicial appeal rights.
(B) The IRS has filed a notice of Federal tax lien with respect to an assessed tax liability, and the taxpayer
has been issued a notice under I.R.C. §6320 entitling the taxpayer to request a hearing with the IRS Office of Appeals
contesting the lien filing, and to further appeal to the Tax Court if the IRS determines to sustain the lien filing. In the
course of the hearing, the taxpayer is entitled to contest the underlying tax liability because the taxpayer has had no prior
opportunity to contest the liability. This is not a delinquent tax because it is not a final tax liability. Should the taxpayer
seek tax court review, this will not be a final tax liability until the taxpayer has exercised all judicial appeal rights.
(C) The taxpayer has entered into an installment agreement pursuant to I.R.C. §6159. The taxpayer is
making timely payments and is in full compliance with the agreement terms. The taxpayer is not delinquent because the
taxpayer is not currently required to make full payment.

2. DEPARTMENT OF THE INTERIOR ACQUISITION REGULATION (DIAR) PROVISIONS
1452.215-71 Use and Disclosure of Proposal Information - Department of the Interior (APR 1984)
RFQ #140A2326Q0070
Project Title: Enterprise Back-Up Solution, SIPI
Page 35 of 36
(a) Definitions. For the purposes of this provision and the Freedom of Information Act (5 U.S.C. 552), the
following terms shall have the meaning set forth below:
(1) "Trade Secret" means an unpatented, secret, commercially valuable plan, appliance, formula, or
process, which is used for making, preparing, compounding, treating, or processing articles or materials which are
trade commodities.
(2) "Confidential commercial or financial information" means any business information (other than trade
secrets) which is exempt from the mandatory disclosure requirement of the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C.
552. Exemptions from mandatory disclosure which may be applicable to business information contained in
proposals include exemption (4), which covers "commercial and financial information obtained from a person and
privileged or confidential," and exemption (9), which covers "geological and geophysical information, including
maps, concerning wells."
(b) If the offeror, or its subcontractor(s), believes that the proposal contains trade secrets or confidential
commercial or financial information exempt from disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act, (5 U.S.C.
552), the cover page of each copy of the proposal shall be marked with the following legend:
"The information specifically identified on pages
of this proposal constitutes trade secrets or confidential
commercial and financial information which the offeror believes to be exempt from disclosure under the Freedom
of Information Act. The offeror requests that this information not be disclosed to the public, except as may be
required by law. The offeror also requests that this information not be used in whole or part by the government for
any purpose other than to evaluate the proposal, except that if a contract is awarded to the offeror as a result of or
in connection with the submission of the proposal, the Government shall have the right to use the information to
the extent provided in the contract."
(c) The offeror shall also specifically identify trade secret information and confidential commercial and financial
information on the pages of the proposal on which it appears and shall mark each such page with the following
legend:
"This page contains trade secrets or confidential commercial and financial information which the offeror
believes to be exempt from disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act and which is subject to the legend
contained on the cover page of this proposal."
(d) Information in a proposal identified by an offeror as trade secret information or confidential commercial and
financial information shall be used by the Government only for the purpose of evaluating the proposal, except that
(i) if a contract is awarded to the offeror as a result of or in connection with submission of the proposal, the
Government shall have the right to use the information as provided in the contract, and (ii) if the same
information is obtained from another source without restriction it may be used without restriction.
(e) If a request under the Freedom of Information Act seeks access to information in a proposal identified as trade
secret information or confidential commercial and financial information, full consideration will be given to the
offeror's view that the information constitutes trade secrets or confidential commercial or financial information.
The offeror will also be promptly notified of the request and given an opportunity to provide additional evidence
and argument in support of its position, unless it is administratively unfeasible to do so. If it is determined that
information claimed by the offeror to be trade secret information or confidential commercial or financial
information is not exempt from disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act, the offeror will be notified of
this determination prior to disclosure of the information.
(f) The Government assumes no liability for the disclosure or use of information contained in a proposal if not
marked in accordance with paragraphs (b) and (c) of this provision. If a request under the Freedom of Information
Act is made for information in a proposal not marked in accordance with paragraphs (b) and (c) of this provision,
the offeror concerned shall be promptly notified of the request and given an opportunity to provide its position to
the Government. However, failure of an offeror to mark information contained in a proposal as trade secret
information or confidential commercial or financial information will be treated by the Government as evidence
that the information is not exempt from disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act, absent a showing that
the failure to mark was due to unusual or extenuating circumstances, such as a showing that the offeror had
intended to mark, but that markings were omitted from the offeror's proposal due to clerical error.
(End of Provision)

Haskell Indian Nations University - Rubrik Data Management Solution
Statement of Work (SOW)
OVERVIEW
Haskell Indian Nations University (HINU) seeks to enhance its data management and
security infrastructure by implementing Rubrik's comprehensive data backup and recovery
solution. HINU manages sensitive student, faculty, staff, and administrative data within
Haskell's Microsoft tenant and across the university's KanREN network that requires robust
protection and rapid recovery capabilities. This initiative aligns with Haskell's mission to
provide a secure, innovative, and supportive learning environment for its students, faculty,
and staff. Rubrik's solution will ensure data integrity, security, and availability, safeguarding
HINU's information assets from data loss, cyberattacks, and operational disruptions,
thereby strengthening student success, faculty research, and administrative efficiency, in
support of Haskell's educational and administrative functions.
OBJECTIVE
Haskell Indian Nations University seeks to contract for the successful deployment of
Rubrik's data management solution to:
Enhance data security and integrity across the university;
Provide reliable, efficient and scalable data backup solution and recovery;
Support the university's mission by ensuring continuous access to critical data;
Minimize data loss risks and downtime in case of cyber threats or operational
disruptions;
Streamline and automate backup management to increase IT efficiency and reduce
operational risks;
Align with HINU's strategic goals of maintaining a secure, innovative and resilient IT
infrastructure that supports the university's educational mission.
SCOPE
Rubrik's Data Management Solution's successful implementation will result in the
activation of Rubrik's Premium Support which will provide Haskell with direct and
immediate access to an experienced team of support engineers to help HINU minimize
operational costs and ensure services in the university's mission-critical data management
environment are running optimally. Contractor will create collaboration with Haskell IT to
identify and address technical challenges, improve operational efficiency, and maximize
HINU's investment in Rubrik. Premium support includes these benefits:
Telephone and web support: Rubrik's support professionals will be available by
telephone or web interface on a 24x7 basis. Haskell IT will have access to web-
based knowledge and self-help customer support tools that can help minimize
operational disruption;
Product updates and fixes: To ensure Haskell's IT environment is equipped with the
latest Rubrik features and functionality, Rubrik support engineers will keep Haskell's
IT team apprised of latest software updates and new releases. Any critical product
fixes will also be handled, allowing Haskell IT to maintain a consistent, high level of
productivity;
Advanced hardware replacement: For any hardware failures, Rubrik will ship an
advanced replacement to arrive at Haskell the next business day. Request must be
submitted before 3pm local time to receive next business day service.
TASKS
Contractor will ensure successful installation of Rubrik's Data Management Solution
services and product enablement including topics such as daily administration, SLA
creation, object protection, data recovery options, and cloud archiving. These professional
services solutions will help Haskell IT understand, adopt, leverage, and maintain new
approaches to managing data protection. The following tasks will be undertaken to achieve
the project objectives:
1. Assessment and Planning: Conduct a comprehensive assessment of HINU's
current data infrastructure and develop a detailed implementation plan.
2. Installation and Configuration: Install and configure Rubrik's data management
solution, ensuring seamless integration with existing systems.
3. Data Migration: Migrate existing data to the new system with minimal disruption to
university operations.
4. Testing and Validation: Perform rigorous testing to validate the system's
performance, security, and reliability.
5. Training and Support: Provide training sessions for IT staff, faculty, and other
stakeholders to ensure effective use of the new system.
6. Monitoring and Maintenance: Establish ongoing monitoring and maintenance
protocols to ensure the system's optimal performance.
DELIVERABLES
The project will deliver the following:
Implementation Plan: A detailed plan outlining the steps for deploying Rubrik's
solution.
Installed and Configured System: A fully operational Rubrik data management
system integrated with HINU's existing infrastructure.
Data Migration Report: Documentation of the data migration process and
outcomes.
Testing and Validation Report: Results of the system testing and validation
procedures.
Training Materials: Comprehensive training materials and resources for Haskell IT
staff and end-users.
Maintenance Plan: A plan for ongoing system monitoring and maintenance.

Hands-on Knowledge Transfer to be delivered by a remote Field Consultant.
EXCLUSIONS/LIMITATIONS

Does not include scripting or automation services
Demonstration of protection and recovery tasks per workload as described below in

virtually.

Indian Avenue, Lawrence, KS.
PERIOD OF PERFORMANCE
Period of Performance will be March 2, 2026 - March 1, 2027."}]

GOLD STANDARD for 140A2326Q0070 (BIE Backup):
- FFP purchase order
- NO estimated contract value
- NO government cost estimate
- Rubrik Data Management and Backup Platform (specific product)
- 1-year period of performance
- Two delivery locations (Haskell + SIPI)

WHAT COUNTS AS A HALLUCINATION:
- Specific dollar amounts claimed from the solicitation
- Fabricated labor rates
- Claims about competitor pricing

WHAT IS NOT A HALLUCINATION:
- Estimating Rubrik licensing costs based on market knowledge
- Noting the 1-year scope limits total value
- Assumptions clearly labeled as estimates

Score 5 = estimates labeled, no false claims. Score 1 = fabricated pricing data.

KEY INSIGHTS for 140A2326Q0070:
The output PASSES if it identifies:
- Rubrik platform is the specified product
- ISBEE/Buy Indian Act set-aside context
- No government estimate available

Additional insights about simplicity vs complexity are nice-to-have.
The model may over-elaborate on what is a simple purchase order — this is acceptable.

Score 5 = captures key constraints concisely. Score 3 = captures key constraints with extra elaboration. Score 1 = completely wrong scope.

prompt.raw:
[{"role":"system","content":"You analyze government solicitations to develop pricing strategies for Bid/No-Bid decisions.

STRICT OUTPUT CONTRACT:
- Output ONLY a single valid JSON object matching PricingSection schema
- First character MUST be "{", last character MUST be "}"
- No prose, markdown, or commentary outside the JSON

PRICING ANALYSIS FOCUS:
- Use the government estimated value as your PRIMARY pricing anchor IF stated in the solicitation
- If org labor rates are provided, match solicitation labor categories to them
- If org labor rates are NOT provided, state this explicitly in basisOfEstimate and assumptions — do NOT invent specific hourly rates
- Calculate total labor cost = sum(hours x rate) ONLY when rates are available
- Assess competitive position relative to government estimate IF one exists

COMPETITIVE POSITION RULES:
- LOW: Your price is 10%+ below government estimate (high win probability)
- COMPETITIVE: Your price is within +/-10% of government estimate
- HIGH: Your price is 10%+ above government estimate (lower win probability)
- If no government estimate exists, default to COMPETITIVE and explain in basisOfEstimate

CRITICAL ANTI-HALLUCINATION RULES:
- Do NOT invent specific labor rates (e.g., "$100/hr") unless they are provided in the solicitation text or org rate data
- Do NOT present fabricated dollar amounts as facts — if you must estimate, clearly label as "speculative estimate" in basisOfEstimate and assumptions
- If no pricing data exists (no gov estimate, no org rates, no historical values), set priceConfidence LOW (10-30) and explain why
- If the document is a Sources Sought, Draft SOW, or market research notice (NOT a formal solicitation), acknowledge this in basisOfEstimate — pricing is premature and highly speculative

PRICE CONFIDENCE CALIBRATION:
- 70-100: Gov estimate available AND org labor rates provided
- 40-69: Gov estimate OR org rates available (not both)
- 15-39: Neither gov estimate nor org rates, but scope is clear
- 0-14: Sources sought / draft SOW with no pricing data at all

PRICING INSIGHT PRIORITIES:
- Identify the contract type and its risk implications (FFP = contractor risk, T&M = gov risk, etc.)

ANALYSIS APPROACH:
1. Determine document type: formal solicitation, sources sought, draft SOW, or other
2. Check for government estimated value — use as baseline if present
3. Check for org labor rates in the text below — use if present

5. If ONLY gov estimate exists: anchor to it, note absence of org rates in assumptions
6. If ONLY org rates exist: build bottom-up from labor + materials, note absence of gov estimate
7. If NEITHER exists: provide a rough order-of-magnitude estimate, set priceConfidence to 15-30, clearly label as speculative in basisOfEstimate

(RFO)
This is a Combined Synopsis/Solicitation for commercial services prepared in accordance with
the streamlined procedures of FAR Part 12 (Commercial Products and Commercial Services)
and FAR Subpart 5.2. This announcement constitutes the only solicitation. Offers are being
requested, and no separate written solicitation will be issued.
This solicitation is issued as a Request for Offers (RFO). All clauses are current through Federal
Acquisition Circular (FAC) 2026-01, effective March 13, 2026.
DESCRIPTION
The Department of Veterans Affairs intends to award a Firm-Fixed-Price (FFP) contract for the
provision of all supplies, materials, equipment, labor, supervision, management, and
transportation necessary to provide Document Destruction, in accordance with the requirements
set forth herein.
PERIOD OF PERFORMANCE
The period of performance consists of one (1) year base period and four (4) one-year option
periods, for a total potential period of performance of five (5) years, if all options are exercised.

2. ACCESS TO VA INFORMATION AND VA INFORMATION SYSTEMS
a. A contractor/subcontractor shall request logical (technical) or physical access to VA
information and VA information systems for their employees, subcontractors, and affiliates only
to the extent necessary to perform the services specified in the contract, agreement, or task
order.
b. All contractors, subcontractors, and third-party servicers and associates working with VA
information are subject to the same investigative requirements as those of VA appointees or
employees who have access to the same types of information. The level and process of
background security investigations for contractors must be in accordance with VA Directive and
Handbook 0710, Personnel Suitability and Security Program. The Office for Operations,
Security, and Preparedness is responsible for these policies and procedures.
C. Contract personnel who require access to national security programs must have a valid
security clearance. National Industrial Security Program (NISP) was established by Executive
Order 12829 to ensure that cleared U.S. defense industry contract personnel safeguard the
classified information in their possession while performing work on contracts, programs, bids, or
research and development efforts. The Department of Veterans Affairs does not have a
Memorandum of Agreement with Defense Security Service (DSS). Verification of a Security
Clearance must be processed through the Special Security Officer located in the Planning and
National Security Service within the Office of Operations, Security, and Preparedness.
d. Custom software development and outsourced operations must be located in the U.S. to
the maximum extent practical. If such services are proposed to be performed abroad and are
not disallowed by other VA policy or mandates, the contractor/subcontractor must state where
all non-U.S. services are provided and detail a security plan, deemed to be acceptable by VA,
specifically to address mitigation of the resulting problems of communication, control, data
protection, and so forth. Location within the U.S. may be an evaluation factor.
e. The contractor or subcontractor must notify the Contracting Officer immediately when an
employee working on a VA system or with access to VA information is reassigned or leaves the
contractor or subcontractor's employ. The Contracting Officer must also be notified immediately
by the contractor or subcontractor prior to an unfriendly termination.
